name: JORDAN Approval Gate — Human-in-the-Loop Security Mechanism for Agent Task Execution
origin_domain: AI agent orchestration (security, human-computer interaction, workflow gating)
progenitor: Aaron (project lead), with the JORDAN/Nexus Fabric development team
driving_insight: Autonomous AI agents that can execute arbitrary tool calls (file operations, shell commands, API requests, code execution) must not have unrestricted authority. Some operations are inherently destructive or privacy-sensitive (file deletion, external POST requests, system configuration changes, credential access), and the AI agent cannot be trusted to correctly assess all risks autonomously — especially when the risk classifier itself can be manipulated via adversarial user input. The approval gate sits between Risk Assessment and Execute as a human-in-the-loop checkpoint: before executing a sub-task classified as HIGH risk, the system pauses and requests explicit human approval. The driving insight is that human judgment is the final authority for dangerous operations, and the system should default to caution (block until approved) for unknowns. However, the implementation betrays this insight: the gate has four independent bypass paths and defaults to LOW on unclassified actions, making it a 'security theater' mechanism rather than a genuine security boundary.
decision_style: Hybrid deliberative-algorithmic with human override. The risk classifier makes an algorithmic decision (regex pattern match) to assign each sub-task a risk level (LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH). The gate then applies a rule: HIGH-risk sub-tasks require human approval (deliberative human decision — approve or reject); LOW and MEDIUM proceed automatically (algorithmic). The gate's decision is binary and per-subtask. This is fundamentally a hierarchical decision structure: the system classifies risk algorithmically, then escalates HIGH-risk decisions to the human authority. The decision style is intended to be restrictive (default deny for dangerous operations), but the implementation defaults to permissive (LOW on no match, bypass paths).
problem_class_targeted: Safety-critical agent task execution where: (1) the agent has access to destructive or privacy-impacting tools (file I/O, shell execution, network requests, code execution); (2) the full risk surface is not known at design time — new tools and new attack vectors emerge; (3) the user (human) is the ultimate authority and bears the consequences of agent actions; (4) false positives (blocking safe operations) degrade user experience and may cause the user to disable the gate; (5) false negatives (allowing dangerous operations) cause actual harm. The problem class is adversarial: both malicious users (trying to weaponize the classifier to approve dangerous operations or to deny service by inflating risk) and incompetent users (submitting accidentally dangerous requests) must be handled correctly.
ordered_phases: The approval gate is a single phase between Risk Assessment and Execute in the JORDAN pipeline. It does not have internal phases — it is itself a phase boundary. The surrounding phases are: (1) RISK ASSESSMENT — classifies each execution sub-task by risk level; (2) APPROVAL GATE (this component) — for HIGH-risk sub-tasks in sequential mode, triggers human approval and pauses execution; (3) EXECUTE — carries out approved sub-tasks. The gate's internal sequence: (a) receive sub-task with risk classification; (b) if risk == LOW or MEDIUM → pass through automatically; (c) if risk == HIGH → create pending approval record, transition state to AWAITING_APPROVAL, notify user; (d) wait for human response (approve/reject); (e) on approval → route sub-task to execute; on rejection → mark sub-task as skipped/rejected. The gate sits on the edge between risk_assessment and execute nodes in the LangGraph compiled graph, enforced by graph topology at the framework level.
feedback_loops: The approval gate has one explicit feedback loop: sub-task rejection loops back to the plan (the rejected sub-task is skipped, and the overall plan may need adjustment). This is not an explicit designed loop but rather a state update — the sub-task's status changes to rejected, and the execution loop skips it. If the rejection invalidates the plan (other sub-tasks depend on the rejected sub-task), the replan node should restructure the plan — but this path is not well-tested. There is no feedback loop from the human decision back to the risk classifier — the classifier does not learn from approval/rejection patterns. The gate has an implicit bypass feedback loop: users who encounter frequent false positives (safe operations blocked) can learn to craft their input to avoid triggering HIGH patterns, effectively breaking the gate through experience — this is an emergent feedback loop between user behavior and classifier evasion.
gates: The approval gate IS the gate — it is the sole mechanism between risk assessment and execution. However, the gate itself has internal bypass paths: (1) C1: the replan → execute edge is unconditional in graph.py:347-348 — after replanning, the revised plan routes directly to execute with NO risk re-check; a workflow that started with LOW-risk sub-tasks can replan into HIGH-risk sub-tasks that execute without human approval; (2) CR1: the parallel executor (_execute_subtask_inline at nodes.py:2072-2092) never calls state.request_approval() — when enable_parallel_execution=True, HIGH-risk sub-tasks execute with zero human review; (3) H4: the planner can unilaterally skip risk assessment via requires_tools=False flag or by marking a task as 'conversational' (gates.py:110-126) — defense-in-depth depends entirely on planner honesty; (4) H3: the direct-response fallback (nodes.py:2728) bypasses subtask-level risk classification — the agent generates a response without any risk classification. These four bypass paths mean the gate is not a security boundary — it is a UX feature that operates under the honor system. The criteria for passing the gate are: sub-task must be classified LOW or MEDIUM (automatic pass), or HIGH with explicit human approval (manual pass). When the gate blocks: the state transitions to AWAITING_APPROVAL, the user is notified, and execution pauses indefinitely until human input arrives.
exit_criteria: The approval gate exits when: (1) all sub-tasks have passed through (either automatic or with human approval) and execution is ready to begin; (2) the user rejects a sub-task — it exits to the execution loop with the sub-task marked as rejected/skipped; (3) the gate is bypassed — execution begins without any approval check (CR1, H4, H3). Failure exits: (1) the user is unavailable — the gate blocks indefinitely; (2) the persistent approval store write fails silently (MR7) — the approval exists in-memory but cannot be resolved via API, creating a split-brain state where the approval is pending but unresolvable.
decomposition_method: The approval gate does not decompose — it evaluates sub-tasks that have already been decomposed by the plan node. However, the gate's granularity (per-subtask) imposes requirements on the decomposition: sub-tasks must be granular enough that a single HIGH-risk tool call does not drag down an entire large sub-task into the approval queue. If sub-tasks are too coarse (many tool calls bundled into one sub-task), a single HIGH-risk tool call within that sub-task gates the entire bundle, creating a coarse-grained approval decision that may not align with the actual risk surface. This creates a tension between decomposition granularity (planner's choice) and approval granularity (gate's scope). The gate does not support per-tool-call approval — it is per-subtask.
granularity_guidance: The gate operates at sub-task granularity — each sub-task is classified and either approved or not. This is a coarse granularity: a sub-task may contain multiple tool calls, and the entire sub-task is gated as a unit. Tool-level risk classification (risk.py classifies individual tool calls) provides finer granularity information, but the gate does not use it — a sub-task is gated as a whole, not tool-by-tool. This mismatch between classification granularity (per tool call) and approval granularity (per sub-task) creates ambiguity: what does it mean to 'approve' a sub-task that contains both LOW-risk and HIGH-risk tool calls? The current implementation resolves this through the first-match-wins pattern (the first HIGH pattern match gates the entire sub-task), but this is a side effect of the pattern ordering, not a designed resolution.
boundary_rules: The gate enforces a hard boundary: no execution (execute node) can begin for a sub-task until the gate has cleared it. In sequential mode, this boundary is enforced by the LangGraph state machine — the state must transition from AWAITING_APPROVAL to EXECUTING before tool calls begin. In parallel mode, this boundary is absent — the inline executor does not check approval state. The boundary between approved and non-approved execution is maintained by the state field awaiting_approval, but: (1) the replan evaluator misreads this field as failure (H1); (2) the persistent store for this field can be out of sync with in-memory state (MR7 split-brain); (3) different code paths reference different approval state representations, creating inconsistent boundary enforcement.
risk_assessment_mechanism: The approval gate relies on the risk classifier (risk.py) for its decisions. The classifier: (1) builds a description string from tool_name + tool_input[:500]; (2) matches against hard-coded regex patterns with re.IGNORECASE; (3) uses first-match-wins ordering; (4) defaults to LOW with confidence 0.7 on no match. The mechanism is described in detail in the JORDAN_v1_Pipeline item. The gate itself does not re-assess risk — it trusts the classifier's output. The gate has a dependency on the classifier: if the classifier is bypassed or manipulated, the gate's decisions are compromised. The four bypass paths (C1, CR1, H4, H3) all operate by either skipping the classifier entirely or executing without consulting the gate. The classifier's weaponizability (CRT1) means that EVEN WHEN the gate is operating correctly, the classification it relies on can be manipulated through user input crafted to match or avoid specific patterns.
unknown_handling: The gate's handling of unknowns is determined by the risk classifier's default: unknown actions (no pattern match) are classified LOW and auto-approved (HR1). This is the most consequential design decision in the entire gate: it means novel, unseen tool calls that are genuinely dangerous but match no existing pattern will execute without approval. The rationale is UX pragmatism — most tool calls are safe, and blocking unknowns would create excessive friction. But the security implication is that the gate provides zero protection against novel attack vectors. The classifier's pattern set is static (hard-coded regex) and does not evolve — new dangerous tools added to the agent's repertoire will slip through until someone manually adds a pattern. This is the classic security trade-off: false positive rate vs. false negative rate, with the gate choosing to minimize false positives (user friction) at the cost of false negatives (missed dangerous operations).
default_stance_on_unknown: PERMISSIVE (allow until blocked). The risk classifier defaults to LOW on no pattern match (HR1), so unknown actions are auto-approved. This is the fundamental philosophical stance of the gate: it is a denylist (block specific known-dangerous patterns), not an allowlist (allow only known-safe patterns). A denylist gate is inherently permissive — it can only block what it has been explicitly told to block. The four bypass paths make the stance even more permissive than the denylist design suggests — even known-dangerous operations can slip through via bypass paths. The gate's PERMISSIVE default is the root cause of the CRT1 finding: because unknown actions are allowed, weaponization requires only evading the known patterns, not gaining approval.
safety_guardrails: The gate itself IS the safety guardrail — it is the only mechanism preventing dangerous tool execution. Auxiliary guardrails: (1) the Nexus sandbox (hermie_run with allowlist, timeout, ulimit) provides defense-in-depth at the shell execution level — if the gate fails, the sandbox may still block dangerous shell commands; (2) the LangGraph state machine provides topological guardrails — nodes cannot be called out of order; (3) the Editor provides a post-hoc quality review. Missing guardrails: no prompt injection defense (user input is directly regex-matched), no rate limiting on approval requests (a malicious task could generate thousands of HIGH-risk sub-tasks, flooding the approval queue), no approval timeout (a pending approval can persist indefinitely, blocking the workflow), no approval audit log (who approved what and when is not systematically recorded), no separation of duties (the same human who submits the task also approves it — no independent reviewer). The critical review recommends 4 specific fixes (Wave 1) to make the gate functional before adding additional guardrails.
human_decision_points: The human is IN the loop at the approval gate — the primary and only human decision point in the JORDAN pipeline. The human is presented with: (1) the sub-task description; (2) the risk level (HIGH); (3) the specific tool calls that triggered the HIGH classification; (4) the choice to approve or reject. The decision is binary (approve/reject) and per-subtask. The human is NOT in the loop for: risk classification decisions (the human does not review LOW/MEDIUM classifications), plan structure decisions (the human does not approve the overall plan), or execution monitoring (the human does not watch sub-tasks execute). The human is effectively a binary gate operator: presented with a stream of approval requests, responding yes/no. This role suffers from alert fatigue if the false positive rate is high — the human learns to approve everything without careful review, defeating the gate's purpose.
override_capability: The human can override at the approval gate: approve (allow execution) or reject (block execution) for each HIGH-risk sub-task. Rejection is an override of the risk classifier's implicit 'this should execute' assumption. The human can also override at the workflow level: stop the entire workflow and restart with modified input, effectively bypassing the gate for the next run. However, the human cannot: (1) reclassify a sub-task (change a HIGH to LOW or vice versa) — only approve or reject; (2) modify a sub-task's tool calls before approval (e.g., 'approve, but don't do the file deletion part'); (3) override a LOW/MEDIUM classification to force approval review (the human cannot say 'I want to review this even though the classifier says it's safe'); (4) intervene mid-execution to halt a running tool call. The override granularity is per-subtask — all-or-nothing at the sub-task level, no per-tool-call granularity.
fallback_when_no_human: When a human is unavailable and a HIGH-risk sub-task awaits approval: the gate blocks indefinitely. The state remains AWAITING_APPROVAL with no timeout, no auto-approve, and no auto-reject. This is the designed behavior: dangerous operations MUST NOT proceed without human approval, and no human means no execution. However, this creates a denial-of-service vector: a malicious or poorly-constructed task that triggers many HIGH-risk classifications will lock up the workflow indefinitely if the human is unavailable. In parallel mode, the question is moot — approval is bypassed, so all sub-tasks execute regardless of human availability. The planner's ability to skip risk assessment (H4) provides a de facto fallback: if the human is unavailable, the planner can mark tasks as requires_tools=False or conversational, bypassing the gate entirely.
max_problem_size: The gate handles one sub-task at a time in sequential mode. The throughput (sub-tasks approved per minute) is bounded by human response time — a human can approve or reject a sub-task in seconds to minutes, yielding a throughput of 10-120 approvals per hour for an attentive human. For large plans with many HIGH-risk sub-tasks, the human becomes the bottleneck. The approval queue can grow unboundedly (all sub-tasks classified before execution begins in the risk assessment phase), and the human must clear the queue before execution proceeds. What breaks at scale: (1) alert fatigue — the human stops reading approval requests carefully and approves everything; (2) queue depth — a task that generates 100+ HIGH-risk sub-tasks is effectively blocked until a human sessions through all of them; (3) the gate is per-workflow, not per-system — multiple concurrent workflows each have their own approval queue, and no mechanism coordinates or prioritizes across queues.
parallel_execution: The approval gate is fundamentally incompatible with the parallel executor as implemented. The parallel executor at nodes.py:2072-2092 never calls state.request_approval(), so all sub-tasks execute without the gate (CR1). This is a deliberate omission in the parallel execution path — the inline executor was implemented independently of the sequential executor, and the approval gating was not ported. To make the gate work with parallel execution, the inline executor would need to: (1) check risk classification before executing each sub-task; (2) for HIGH-risk sub-tasks, pause that specific sub-task (not the entire batch) and request approval; (3) short-circuit the batch loop when any sub-task has awaiting_approval=True. This would require the parallel executor to support partial execution (some sub-tasks run while others await approval), which the current implementation does not support — it executes all ready tasks in each batch synchronously.
team_scaling_model: The gate is designed for single-human, single-workflow operation. There is no mechanism for: (1) routing approvals to different humans based on sub-task type or risk level; (2) multi-human approval (requiring two humans to approve the same sub-task); (3) role-based approval (only certain humans can approve certain categories of operations); (4) approval delegation (human A can delegate approval authority to human B). The human approver is implicitly the same person who submitted the task — there is no independent review. In a team setting, this means the person most invested in the task completing (the submitter) is also the person deciding whether it is safe — a conflict of interest.
cost_scaling_model: The human's time is the primary cost, and it scales linearly with the number of HIGH-risk sub-tasks × the human's time per approval decision. Each approval request imposes a context-switching cost on the human: they must read the sub-task description, understand the risk, and decide. This cognitive cost is approximately constant per sub-task. Token cost is negligible (the gate does not invoke LLMs). The cost scaling model reveals a tension: making the gate more strict (classifying more sub-tasks as HIGH) increases human cost linearly, creating pressure to keep the gate loose (classify as LOW by default) to minimize human burden. The current LOW-default design (HR1) is a direct response to this cost pressure — the gate is tuned for minimal human involvement, not maximal security. A more sophisticated gate would use adaptive thresholds: increase strictness when human attention is available, decrease when the human is overloaded.
replanning_built_in: The gate interacts with replanning through the C1 finding: replan routes directly to execute, bypassing the gate. This means that sub-tasks created or restructured during replanning are never risk-classified or gated. The replan-to-execute edge is unconditional in graph.py:347-348. The fix is to route replan → risk_assessment → execute instead, ensuring replanned sub-tasks go through the same risk classification and approval gating as original sub-tasks. Additionally, H1 creates a replanning interaction: the replan evaluator misreads approval-pending sub-tasks as failures, triggering replanning when the only issue is that the human has not responded yet. This causes replanning to try to 'fix' sub-tasks that are not broken, just awaiting approval.
trigger_mechanism: The gate is triggered by the completion of risk assessment — all sub-tasks have been classified, and those classified HIGH are queued for approval. The approval process is triggered by the workflow reaching the gate's position in the graph topology (the edge between risk_assessment and execute nodes). In sequential mode, the gate is triggered once after risk assessment and persists until all approvals are resolved. In parallel mode, the gate is never triggered (bypass). The human's approval decision is triggered by the system notification (the state transition to AWAITING_APPROVAL generates a user-visible message).
adaptation_cost: When the gate blocks (sub-task awaits approval), the cost is the human's decision latency. When the gate is bypassed, the cost is the risk of executing dangerous operations — a probabilistic cost (probability of harm × magnitude of harm). The four bypass paths mean this risk is non-trivial and unquantified. When the gate generates false positives (blocks safe operations), the cost is user frustration and workflow delay — the approval queue grows, and the user must manually approve operations they know are safe. This creates a perverse incentive: users learn to craft inputs that avoid triggering HIGH patterns, effectively performing adversarial evasion against their own security gate to reduce friction.
failure_recovery_mechanism: The gate's failure recovery is limited: (1) If a sub-task is incorrectly classified HIGH (false positive), the human can approve it and execution proceeds — recovery is manual and per-instance; (2) If a sub-task is incorrectly classified LOW (false negative), there is no recovery — the dangerous operation executes without approval; (3) If the persistent approval store write fails (MR7 split-brain), the approval is in-memory but unresolvable via API — recovery requires restarting the workflow; (4) If the gate is bypassed (C1, CR1, H3, H4), there is no recovery — the bypass is invisible to the gate, and the unsafe execution proceeds; (5) If the human rejects a sub-task, the execution loop supposedly skips it, but H2 reveals the skip flag is never checked — recovery from rejection may not work as intended, leaving the workflow in an inconsistent state. There is no automatic recovery — all recovery requires human intervention or workflow restart.
output_artifacts: The gate produces: (1) approval decisions (approve/reject) per gated sub-task, stored in the workflow state; (2) an approval queue (pending approvals visible to the user); (3) an approval audit trail (which sub-tasks were approved, which were rejected, timestamps — but MR7 makes this unreliable due to split-brain between in-memory and persistent storage); (4) user notifications (the AWAITING_APPROVAL state transition generates a message to the user). The gate does not produce: (1) a summary of what was auto-approved (LOW/MEDIUM sub-tasks) — only HIGH ones are reported; (2) a risk justification (why was this classified HIGH? which pattern matched?); (3) a safety dashboard (overall risk posture of the workflow).
briefing_format: The approval request is presented to the human as a natural language description: the sub-task name/description, the risk level (HIGH), and the tool calls that will be made. This is an LLM-generated narrative, not a structured format. The lack of standardized briefing means the human must parse natural language to understand what they are approving — a structured format (tool name, parameters, risk justification, blast radius estimate) would enable faster and more accurate decisions. The briefing does not include: the specific regex pattern that triggered the HIGH classification, alternative safer approaches, or the consequences of rejection.
traceability: Moderate traceability: the gate records approval decisions in the workflow state, and these decisions flow into the LangGraph checkpoint. You can trace from an execution event back to whether it was approved and by whom. However, traceability gaps: (1) MR7 split-brain — the persistent store may be out of sync with in-memory state, so the recorded approval decision may not match what actually happened; (2) bypass paths are invisible — there is no trace that a sub-task executed without approval because the trace shows it as 'executed', not 'executed via bypass'; (3) the risk classifier's decision rationale (which pattern matched) is not recorded alongside the classification, so you cannot trace from an approval back to WHY the sub-task was classified HIGH; (4) CPT1 weaponized classifications are indistinguishable from legitimate ones in the trace — you cannot tell whether the user crafted input to evade or inflate risk. The S3 thread-ID inconsistency further breaks traceability for approval events.
agent_communication_protocol: The gate communicates with the human via the JORDAN agent's response channel (the same channel that delivers task results). The approval request is embedded in the agent's natural language output — there is no separate approval API, webhook, or push notification. This means the human must be actively watching the agent's output to see approval requests. The gate does not communicate with other agents — it is a human-only checkpoint. Within the Nexus Fabric, the gate could in principle use Nexus's inter-agent communication protocol to request a human review from a designated human proxy agent, but this is not implemented.
learning_memory_architecture: The gate has no learning or memory. Each approval decision is independent — the gate does not: (1) learn from approval patterns (if the human always approves sub-tasks matching pattern X, the gate could reclassify them as MEDIUM); (2) remember previous decisions for similar sub-tasks (you approved this database migration last time — approve again?); (3) adapt classification thresholds based on the human's demonstrated risk tolerance; (4) share risk intelligence across workflows (sub-task type X was classified HIGH in workflow A, so it should also be HIGH in workflow B). The static regex patterns represent frozen organizational knowledge about what is dangerous, but they do not evolve based on operational experience. A learning gate would: (1) observe approval/rejection patterns and suggest pattern updates; (2) personalize thresholds per user; (3) escalate novel patterns to human review for addition to the pattern set.
latency_bounds: The gate introduces unbounded latency: the human can take seconds, minutes, hours, or days to respond to an approval request. The workflow is blocked during this time. There is no SLA, no timeout, and no automatic escalation. For interactive use cases, this is acceptable (the user is present and can respond immediately). For batch/long-running use cases, this is problematic — a workflow that runs overnight may be blocked at the first HIGH-risk sub-task and make zero progress until morning. The gate's latency is entirely determined by human response time, which is unpredictable. In parallel mode, the gate introduces zero latency (because it is bypassed) — but this comes at the cost of zero security.
evaluation_benchmarking: The approval gate has been evaluated through adversarial code review (jordan-critical-review.md) but not through quantitative metrics. The review found: 4 bypass paths (C1, CR1, H3, H4), a weaponizable classifier (CRT1), false positive patterns (HR2), and a permissive default (HR1). No metrics exist for: false positive rate (what fraction of gated sub-tasks are actually safe?), false negative rate (what fraction of executed sub-tasks should have been gated?), approval latency distribution, human error rate in approval decisions, or bypass rate (what fraction of sub-tasks take a bypass path?). The gate's effectiveness is assumed, not measured. Standard security evaluation methodologies (penetration testing, red teaming, threat modeling) have not been systematically applied.
mapping_strength: The JORDAN approval gate maps directly to the broader concept of human-in-the-loop AI safety gates. It is the reference implementation of this concept in the JORDAN pipeline. The gate's design pattern (risk-classify the pipeline classify, gate on threshold, escalate to human) is generalizable to any AI system that executes actions with safety implications.
gaps: Self-analysis gaps (from the critical review): (1) the gate is a single point of security failure with four independent bypass paths — it does not provide defense-in-depth; (2) the risk classifier it depends on is weaponizable (CRT1), making the gate's decisions unreliable even when operating correctly; (3) the permissive default (LOW on unknown) makes the gate a denylist rather than an allowlist, inverting the security principle of default-deny; (4) false positives from the first-match-wins regex (HR2) create alert fatigue that trains users to approve without reading; (5) there is no approval audit trail that survives the split-brain (MR7) — compliance and forensic analysis are impossible; (6) the gate is bypassed in parallel mode (CR1), making it incompatible with the performance path; (7) replanned sub-tasks skip the gate (C1), creating a temporal bypass — a task that was safe when planned may be unsafe after replanning; (8) the gate operates at sub-task granularity, not tool-call granularity — a sub-task containing multiple tool calls of different risk levels forces an all-or-nothing decision; (9) the planner can skip the gate (H4), making the gate dependent on the honesty of another LLM component — a compromised or hallucinating planner bypasses the gate; (10) there is no mechanism to test the gate's effectiveness quantitatively.
adaptation_required: Already the reference implementation. Wave 1 fixes from the critical review: (1) C1: change replan → execute to replan → risk_assessment (conditional edge, 2 lines); (2) CR1: port request_approval() to _execute_subtask_inline with batch loop short-circuit (~20 lines); (3) H4: remove requires_tools=False bypass — classify all subtasks regardless of planner annotation (~10 lines); (4) CRT1: separate tool-behavior pattern match from user-input pattern match; change default from LOW to MEDIUM/UNCLASSIFIED (~30 lines). Additional improvements: (5) add approval timeout (auto-reject or escalate after configurable period); (6) add per-user approval history and pattern learning; (7) add structured approval briefing format; (8) add quantitative gate effectiveness metrics (false positive/negative rate tracking); (9) implement defense-in-depth (Nexus sandbox as a second layer of protection).
validation_context: Validated through dual-agent adversarial code review (Richard + Karl, May 2026) which identified 6 CRITICAL, 12 HIGH, and 10 SERIOUS findings across the full codebase. The approval gate was specifically targeted in the review as the primary security boundary. The review methodology involved independent line-by-line analysis by two different AI agents (Claude Code and Gemini CLI) with findings cross-validated by a human orchestrator (Hermes). No quantitative validation (benchmarks, penetration tests, user studies) has been conducted.
known_failures: Documented bypass paths: (1) C1: replan unconditionally routes to execute without risk re-check (graph.py:347-348); (2) CR1: parallel executor never calls request_approval() (nodes.py:2072-2092); (3) H4: planner unilaterally skips risk via requires_tools=False or conversational category (gates.py:110-126); (4) H3: direct-response fallback bypasses subtask-level classification (nodes.py:2728). Classifier failures: (5) CRT1: user input weaponizes classification — 'delete all passwords' in a search query → HIGH; 'wipe entries' → LOW default; (6) HR1: default-to-LOW on no pattern match with confidence 0.7; (7) HR2: first-match-wins with re.IGNORECASE causes false positives — 'Document the password reset flow' → HIGH. State failures: (8) MR7: create_pending_approval split-brain — in-memory queue succeeds, persistent store write fails silently; (9) H1: replan misreads approval-pending as failure; (10) H2: replan 'skip' flag never checked by execution loop. The gate can be defeated by: crafting user input to avoid pattern matches, using parallel mode, triggering replanning, or using conversational task categories.
where_still_used: Active component of the JORDAN v1 pipeline under development in the iain workspace. The gate is operational in sequential mode with the documented vulnerabilities present. The Wave 1 security fixes are planned but not yet implemented as of the critical review date (May 2026).
